chain_of_thought: Top native frame is Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum inside libUbikeyJni.so, below any allocator/sanitizer frames. No detailed decompilation is available due to 404 errors when searching functions, but symbol names and stack strongly suggest X.509/ASN.1 parsing helpers used by CERT_Load and Convert_ASN1_to_X509_* routines.

The JNI bridge Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetPolicyOID takes a byte[] from Java and likely passes it (directly or via CERT_Load) into the certificate/ASN.1 parsing pipeline. The fuzz harness fuzz_one_input substitutes for normal Java entry, but in production the same native stack would be reachable from app code (UbikeyJni.jGetPolicyOID).

Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum is almost certainly computing the number or index of children in an ASN.1 structure. Crashes at this point in such functions are typically due to malformed lengths, out-of-bounds indexing, or walking past the end of a buffer when interpreting untrusted DER/ASN.1 data. Given standard patterns, this is unlikely to be a deliberate abort with validation and more likely an unchecked trust in ASN.1 length/structure fields.

Attacker control: the certificate/ASN.1 bytes come from Java-provided byte[]; in a smart-billing / ubikey context they are plausibly remote (e.g., server certs, policy OIDs from network or QR). There is no evidence of strong validation before native parsing. Fuzzer was able to crash it via malformed input, which likely mimics what a malicious remote endpoint or file could do.

Because we lack exact C code, we cannot pinpoint memcpy parameters or exact arithmetic, but a crash inside a low-level ASN.1 child-counting routine strongly indicates unsafe parsing of untrusted ASN.1, which is a classic cause of memory-safety bugs. This suggests at least a robust denial-of-service and potentially more (over-reads/writes) if miscalculated lengths drive subsequent memory accesses.

However, without a precise crash reason (SIGSEGV vs. abort) and without decompiled body, we must be slightly conservative: label as likely vulnerability with memory-safety concerns, not fully confirmed. Severity is set to high due to typical impact of ASN.1 parsing bugs (DoS and possible RCE), but not critical given missing proof of control-flow hijack.

The harness itself (fuzz_one_input) is not doing obviously unrealistic things such as forcing null pointers or violating ownership; it appears to feed arbitrary certificate bytes into the same JNI path the app would use. Thus we should not attribute the crash to harness/environment but to native parsing logic exposed by jGetPolicyOID.
reasons: Crash occurs in Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum, a low-level ASN.1 parser helper, during processing of attacker-controllable certificate bytes.
Input byte[] flows from Java (UbikeyJni.jGetPolicyOID) into CERT_Load and ASN.1 conversion without evidence of strict validation, enabling malformed ASN.1 to influence parsing logic.

Such functions commonly rely on internal length/index calculations; a crash indicates likely out-of-bounds access or other memory-safety issue in ASN.1 child counting rather than a benign, handled error path.
The fuzz harness mimics realistic usage by passing arbitrary certificate-like data; no signs the crash is purely due to harness-only misuse like null handles or impossible JNI contracts.
recommendations: Manually decompile and review Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum and related ASN.1 parsing functions to identify unchecked length/index arithmetic and enforce strict bounds checks before accessing buffers.

Add robust ASN.1 structural validation (length, tag, and child counts) before using values to index into arrays or recurse into sub-elements; on any inconsistency, abort parsing gracefully without dereferencing pointers.

Augment fuzzing with ASan/UBSan-instrumented builds of libUbikeyJni.so focused on certificate/ASN.1 inputs to confirm and characterize out-of-bounds or use-after-free conditions and develop regression tests.
assumptions: UbikeyJni.jGetPolicyOID takes attacker-influenced or at least externally provided certificate/ASN.1 data (e.g., from network or files).
Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum does not intentionally abort on benign malformed input but crashes due to unsafe parsing.
The fuzz harness uses the same JNI code path and argument types as the production app when calling jGetPolicyOID.
limitations: Ghidra MCP search for Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum and JNI entry function returned HTTP 404, preventing direct decompilation of the native code.
Exact crash type (signal, faulting address, allocator involvement) is not provided, limiting precision on whether this is out-of-bounds read vs. write vs. null deref.
Java-side code for UbikeyJni.jGetPolicyOID was seen only by signature, not full body, so early Java validation cannot be fully ruled out, though unlikely for ASN.1 structural correctness.

exploit.trigger_method: Supplying a maliciously crafted certificate/ASN.1 blob to UbikeyJni.jGetPolicyOID via its Java entrypoint
prerequisites: Attacker can influence the certificate or policy data that the app passes into UbikeyJni.jGetPolicyOID (e.g., via network endpoint, QR/file import, or MITM of a TLS-like channel).
libUbikeyJni.so is present and used by the targeted app version without additional sandboxing or broker validation.
exploit_pipeline: Identify a code path in the app where untrusted or semi-untrusted certificate/ASN.1 data is passed into UbikeyJni.jGetPolicyOID (e.g., by reversing app network/file handling).
Craft ASN.1/DER input that triggers malformed child structures so that Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum miscalculates child counts or indexes, provoking the same crash observed under fuzzing.
Deliver the crafted payload through the chosen vector (malicious server response, tampered file/QR, or IPC) so the app invokes UbikeyJni.jGetPolicyOID with the malicious byte[].
Observe app crash or abnormal behavior; iterate with targeted fuzzing and analysis (with sanitizers) to refine payload for potential memory corruption beyond DoS.
poc_commands:
# Assuming an activity or service that eventually calls UbikeyJni.jGetPolicyOID with a file path extra
adb push crafted_cert.der /sdcard/Download/payload.der
adb shell am start -n <package>/<activity> --es cert_path /sdcard/Download/payload.der
exploit.notes: Because app component names and exact data flow paths are unknown, the PoC command uses placeholders. A practical PoC would be built after locating where UbikeyJni.jGetPolicyOID is invoked with external data (e.g., certificate import, policy verification).

chain_of_thought: Top native frame is __memcpy, next is Decode_DER_ASN1_ST within libUbikeyJni.so, followed by Get_DER_Child, Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT, CERT_Load, and the JNI bridge Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetPolicyOID. __memcpy suggests a buffer copy; typical crash causes are OOB read/write or invalid pointer.

We attempted to inspect the native functions in libUbikeyJni.so via Ghidra MCP (open_lib succeeded, but search_functions_by_name calls failed with HTTP 404 from the analysis backend), so we cannot see the decompiled bodies of Decode_DER_ASN1_ST, Get_DER_Child, etc. That means we must infer behavior from naming and stack context only.

Decode_DER_ASN1_ST / Get_DER_Child / Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT / CERT_Load form a plausible ASN.1 / X.509 parsing pipeline: CERT_Load likely takes a DER-encoded certificate, Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT builds an internal structure, Get_DER_Child navigates ASN.1 TLV children, and Decode_DER_ASN1_ST decodes specific fields. __memcpy at the top strongly hints that decoding logic is copying content out of the DER blob into fixed or computed buffers.

Because this comes from a fuzzing harness fuzz_one_input into the JNI method, we know the DER input (certificate or raw ASN.1) is attacker-controlled under fuzz; in a real app, this would typically be a certificate received from a server, file, or NFC token. Thus, lengths and offsets used in ASN.1 parsing are a direct function of untrusted bytes.

For ASN.1/X.509 parsing code, a crash in memcpy is usually due to using length/offset values from the DER structure without complete bounds checking (e.g., trusting a length field that extends past the end of the buffer or copying more data than the destination buffer can hold). That is a classic memory safety bug: out-of-bounds read/write or buffer overflow. The stack does not show any sanitizer abort frame; the termination reason is just __memcpy, not an explicit abort() due to a harness check, so allocator/sanitizer interference is unlikely to be the primary cause.

Without decompiled code we cannot point to the exact line, but the structure (Decode_DER_ASN1_ST → memcpy) is enough to infer that the memcpy destination or length is derived from ASN.1 tag/length parsing. Unless there is comprehensive length validation in each stage, malformed DER can cause copying past buffer boundaries. The fuzz crash indicates such validation is missing or incomplete for at least one path.

There is no sign this is a harness-only artifact (e.g., NULL env or invalid JNI usage). The stack shows a realistic JNI bridge, then CERT_Load and normal parsing utilities. Fuzz harness merely replaces the usual Java entrypoint but still exercises real library logic. Thus, the condition is realistically reachable when the app processes a crafted certificate or ASN.1 blob from the network or other untrusted source.

Because the crash is at memcpy with ASN.1 length handling, I classify this as a likely out-of-bounds access / buffer overflow vulnerability in libUbikeyJni.so's certificate parsing. Impact is at least process crash (DoS) and likely memory corruption with potential for code execution, depending on whether the overflow is a write into heap/stack. Lacking exact code, I conservatively mark severity as high and exploitability as theoretical but plausible.

Call sequence is: fuzz_one_input → Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetPolicyOID → CERT_Load → Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT → Get_DER_Child → Decode_DER_ASN1_ST → __memcpy. This is a clean taint path from attacker-controlled DER input to memcpy length/dest without proven sanitization, supporting a vulnerability classification.
reasons: Crash occurs in __memcpy during ASN.1/X.509 decoding functions (Decode_DER_ASN1_ST, Get_DER_Child) suggesting unsafe copy based on parsed lengths
Input to CERT_Load originates from fuzzed/untrusted data passed into JNI bridge Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetPolicyOID, so DER length/offset fields are attacker-controlled
No evidence of robust bounds checking before memcpy; classic pattern for OOB read/write in certificate/ASN.1 parsers
Crash path goes through real library logic, not a harness-specific misuse of JNI APIs
recommendations: Manually audit CERT_Load, Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT, Get_DER_Child, and Decode_DER_ASN1_ST for all memcpy/memmove calls and ensure destination buffers and lengths are validated against the actual DER buffer size
Introduce strict bounds checking when parsing ASN.1 length fields (reject or clamp fields that extend beyond the input buffer or destination structure sizes)
Add defensive checks in JNI layer for maximum acceptable certificate size and use safe wrappers for memcpy (e.g., memmove_s-style or explicit size checks)
Enable ASan/UBSan builds of libUbikeyJni.so and re-run fuzzing to identify any additional similar issues in the ASN.1 parsing pipeline
assumptions: JNI bridge Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetPolicyOID ultimately receives a DER-encoded certificate or ASN.1 blob from untrusted sources in real use (e.g., network, file, token)
Decode_DER_ASN1_ST uses memcpy with lengths derived from parsed ASN.1 fields without full validation in at least one path
The fuzz crash corresponds to an out-of-bounds access in memcpy, not an artificial harness constraint like forced abort
limitations: Ghidra MCP backend returned 404 for search_functions_by_name, so exact decompiled code for Decode_DER_ASN1_ST/CERT_Load could not be inspected
No direct view of Java-side construction/validation of inputs to jGetPolicyOID, so untrusted-source assumptions are inferred from typical certificate-handling flows
We cannot distinguish precisely whether the memcpy overflow is on read or write side, so RCE feasibility is estimated, not proven

exploit.trigger_method: Supplying a crafted DER-encoded X.509 certificate/ASN.1 blob processed via UbikeyJni.jGetPolicyOID
prerequisites: Attacker can cause the app to process an untrusted certificate or ASN.1-encoded policy object (e.g., via network connection, file, or NFC/QR workflow)
libUbikeyJni.so is used in the production app for parsing these inputs
No additional sandboxing prevents process compromise from native memory corruption
exploit_pipeline: Craft a DER-encoded X.509 certificate or ASN.1 blob with malformed length fields that cause Decode_DER_ASN1_ST to compute a memcpy length larger than the available buffer
Deliver the crafted certificate/blob to the target app through whatever interface feeds into UbikeyJni.jGetPolicyOID (e.g., TLS handshake, imported file, smart card, or other identity token)
Trigger the Java code path that invokes UbikeyJni.jGetPolicyOID so that CERT_Load and subsequent ASN.1 parsing are executed on attacker-controlled data

Upon processing, the malformed lengths cause __memcpy in Decode_DER_ASN1_ST to perform an out-of-bounds copy, corrupting memory and at minimum crashing the app; with careful shaping, this may be turned into controlled heap/stack corruption
poc_commands:
# Push crafted payload (certificate or ASN.1 blob) to device
adb push crafted_policy.der /sdcard/Download/crafted_policy.der

# Start target app activity that loads a policy/certificate (placeholder component)
adb shell am start -n com.skt.smartbill/.MainActivity --es cert_path "/sdcard/Download/crafted_policy.der"
exploit.notes: Exact Java entrypoint and intent extras are unknown; PoC commands assume there is an activity or service that reads a certificate file and ultimately calls UbikeyJni.jGetPolicyOID. The crafted DER must be tuned (e.g., via fuzzing) to reproduce the memcpy crash reliably and may be further refined for exploitation.

chain_of_thought: Stack shows memcpy at top; first app-level frame below it is Decode_DER_ASN1_ST, then CERT_Load, then JNI bridge Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetPolicyOID; all in libUbikeyJni.so per lib map.
Tooling could not decompile individual functions due to HTTP 404 errors from MCP, so I must reason from symbol names and general patterns rather than exact C code; I note this as a limitation.

A crash in __memcpy almost always indicates out-of-bounds read/write or invalid pointer, unless memcpy is passed totally bogus pointers from harness; here the fuzzer entry is fuzz_one_input, which likely controls a byte buffer passed down to JNI/DER decoding routines.

Functions named Decode_DER_ASN1_ST and CERT_Load strongly suggest ASN.1/DER parsing of certificates or policies, consistent with the JNI method name jGetPolicyOID; policy OID is an ASN.1 object identifier field in X.509 certs.

Typical vulnerable pattern: parse DER structure, compute length/offsets from untrusted data, then memcpy fields into fixed-size buffers or heap buffers without thorough bounds checks; errors in length validation lead to memcpy overruns causing crashes in memcpy.

Because JNI bridge jGetPolicyOID presumably receives some kind of certificate or DER blob from Java (possibly from external sources like files/network), attacker control of the ASN.1 input is plausible in a real app context (e.g., scanning smart card, external cert, etc.).

The fuzzer harness feeding arbitrary inputs mimics attacker-controlled ASN.1, and the fact the crash is specifically in Decode_DER_ASN1_ST/CERT_Load rather than in fuzz harness suggests the library lacks robust defensive parsing; if there were strong length guards, random fuzz input would more often cause parse failures instead of memcpy crashes.

I cannot see the exact memcpy callsite or confirm if dst or len is constant, but from naming, Decode_DER_ASN1_ST is almost certainly decoding arbitrary DER tags into internal structs, which by definition uses length values embedded in the input; incorrect or missing validation of those implicit length fields maps directly to CWE-787 (out-of-bounds write) or CWE-125 (read) via memcpy.

No evidence indicates this is a harness-only misuse such as passing NULL env or impossible object types; the crash path stays entirely inside the app library from JNI down to parsing functions, which is exactly the code that would execute in production if given malformed input.

Given the above, the most reasonable classification is that this represents a genuine and likely exploitable memory-safety bug in the certificate/DER decoding path reachable from Java through jGetPolicyOID, though details (heap vs stack, read vs write) are unknown; impact is at least high due to potential for controlled overflows, but without allocator details I avoid calling it critical.

Confidence is not maximal (not 0.9+) because I lack decompiled code and precise argument tracking, but naming plus memcpy crash in a decoder heavily suggest a real vulnerability rather than benign abort or harness-only fault.
reasons: Crash occurs in __memcpy called from Decode_DER_ASN1_ST/CERT_Load, indicating memory misuse during ASN.1/DER decoding.

Decode_DER_ASN1_ST and CERT_Load almost certainly derive memcpy length and offsets from attacker-controlled DER/ASN.1 fields, and fuzzing caused a hard crash rather than graceful parse failure, implying missing or insufficient bounds checks.
JNI bridge Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetPolicyOID is a normal app entry and will be reachable with untrusted certificate/policy inputs in production, not just from the fuzz harness.
recommendations: Decompile Decode_DER_ASN1_ST and CERT_Load to locate all memcpy/memmove calls and add strict bounds checks based on actual buffer sizes, not just DER lengths.
Introduce robust DER/ASN.1 validation that rejects any structure where claimed lengths exceed remaining buffer size before performing copies or allocations.
Add fuzz-driven tests for jGetPolicyOID and certificate loading paths, and run under ASan or similar to confirm no further out-of-bounds reads/writes occur.
assumptions: Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetPolicyOID accepts certificate or policy data that can originate from attacker-controlled sources (files, network, etc.).
Decode_DER_ASN1_ST uses length and offset information directly from the DER input to drive memcpy, without full boundary checks on all paths.
The fuzz harness passes raw byte data to the same native APIs in a way that is representative of production usage, just with more extreme inputs.
limitations: Unable to retrieve decompiled code for Decode_DER_ASN1_ST, CERT_Load, or the JNI bridge due to MCP HTTP 404 errors.
Exact nature of the memory error (out-of-bounds read vs write, heap vs stack) and whether the crash is consistently reproducible with controlled payloads is unknown.
No visibility into Java-side argument validation or how certificates/policies are sourced in the real app.

exploit.trigger_method: Malicious or malformed certificate/policy data passed to UbikeyJni.jGetPolicyOID via normal app interfaces
prerequisites: Attacker can supply or influence certificate/policy data processed by the app (e.g., via file input, network response, or external token).
App invokes UbikeyJni.jGetPolicyOID or related certificate loading path on attacker-supplied data.
No additional hardening (e.g., robust length checks added) is present in shipping version.
exploit_pipeline: Craft a DER-encoded certificate or policy blob with inconsistent or oversized length fields in the ASN.1 structure targeting the field decoded by Decode_DER_ASN1_ST.
Deliver this blob to the app using any available mechanism (e.g., import certificate, open file, or trigger network path) that ultimately calls UbikeyJni.jGetPolicyOID.
When the app processes the blob, CERT_Load invokes Decode_DER_ASN1_ST, which uses the attacker-controlled length to call memcpy, leading to an out-of-bounds access and process crash.
With careful tuning and analysis of heap layout, attempt to shape the overflow (if it is a write) to corrupt adjacent heap metadata or control structures, potentially enabling more than a simple DoS.
exploit.notes: The exact field and overflow direction (read vs write) require code-level confirmation; PoC payload structure will depend on the specific ASN.1 layout expected by CERT_Load/Decode_DER_ASN1_ST.
